content: Which of the following statements is/are TRUE with respect to an ideal opamp?

options.content: It has an infinite input resistance
It has an infinite output resistance
It has an infinite open-loop differential gain
It has an infinite open-loop common-mode gain

explanation:
An ideal op‐amp is defined by these key properties:

Infinite input resistance: $$R_{\rm in}\to\infty$$

Zero output resistance: $$R_{\rm out}=0$$

Infinite open‐loop differential gain: $$A_{\rm d}\to\infty$$

Zero open‐loop common‐mode gain: $$A_{\rm cm}=0$$

Mapping these to the options:

A (infinite input resistance) – True

B (infinite output resistance) – False (it’s zero)

C (infinite open‐loop differential gain) – True

D (infinite open‐loop common‐mode gain) – False (it’s zero)

So the true statements are A and C.

content: An ideal OPAMP circuit with a sinusoidal input is shown in the figure. The 3 dB frequency is the frequency at which the magnitude of the voltage gain decreases by 3 dB from the maximum value. Which of the options is/are correct?

options.content: The circuit is a low pass filter.
The circuit is a high pass filter.

explanation:
Given circuit is a high pass filter.

$${\omega _c} = {1 \over {{R_1}{C_1}}} = {1 \over {{{10}^3} \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}} = 1000$$ rad/sec.

Hence, options (b) and (c) are correct.

content:
For the circuit with an ideal OPAMP shown in the figure. $V_{\text {REF }}$ is fixed.

If $V_{\text {OUT }}=1$ volt for $V_{\text {IN }}=0.1$ volt and $V_{\text {OUT }}=6$ volt, for $V_{\text {IN }}=1 \mathrm{~V}$, where $V_{\text {OUT }}$ is measured across $R_{\mathrm{L}}$ connected at the output of this OPAMP. The value of $R_{\mathrm{F}} / R_{\text {IN }}$ is
explanation:
The given circuit is shown below,

Given :

(i) $V_{\text {out }}=1 \mathrm{~V}$ when $V_{\text {in }}=0.1 \mathrm{~V}$

(ii) $V_{\text {out }}=6 \mathrm{~V}$ when $V_{\text {in }}=1 \mathrm{~V}$

By voltage divider rule, voltage at non-inverting terminal can be found as

Thus,

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_{+}=\left(\frac{R}{R+R}\right) V_{r e f} \\ & V_{+}=\frac{V_{r e f}}{2} \end{aligned} $$

Here virtual ground concept is applicable because-ve feedback is present and op-amp is ideal so due to virtual ground concept,

$$ V_{-}=V_{+}=\frac{V_{r e f}}{2} $$

Applying KCL at $V_{-}$,

$$ \frac{V_{-}-V_{i n}}{R_{i n}}+\frac{V_{-}-V_{o u t}}{R_F}=0 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{V_{\text {ref }}}{2}-V_{\text {in }} \\ & \frac{R_{\text {in }}}{2}+\frac{\frac{V_{\text {ref }}}{2}-V_{\text {out }}}{R_F}=0 \\ & \frac{V_{\text {ref }}}{2}\left[\frac{1}{R_{\text {in }}}+\frac{1}{R_F}\right]-\frac{V_{\text {in }}}{R_{\text {in }}}=\frac{V_{\text {out }}}{R_F} \\ & \frac{V_{\text {ref }}}{2}\left[\frac{R_{\text {in }}+R_F}{R_{\text {in }} R_F}\right]-\frac{V_{\text {in }}}{R_{\text {in }}}=\frac{V_{\text {out }}}{R_F} \\ & \frac{V_{\text {ref }}}{2}\left[1+\frac{R_F}{R_{\text {in }}}\right]-\left(\frac{R_F}{R_{\text {in }}}\right) V_{\text {in }}=V_{\text {out }} .........(i)\end{aligned} $$

Condition 1: If $V_{\text {out }}=1 \mathrm{~V}$ when $V_{\text {in }}=0.1 \mathrm{~V}$ then equation (i) becomes as

$$ \frac{V_{r e f}}{2}\left[1+\frac{R_F}{R_{i n}}\right]-(0.1)\left(\frac{R_F}{R_{i n}}\right)=1 $$

Condition 2 : If $V_{\text {out }}=6 \mathrm{~V}$ when $V_{\text {in }}=1 \mathrm{~V}$ then equation (ii) becomes as

$$ \frac{V_{r e f}}{2}\left[1+\frac{R_F}{R_{i n}}\right]-(1)\left(\frac{R_F}{R_{i n}}\right)=6 $$

Assume $\frac{V_{r e f}}{2}\left[1+\frac{R_F}{R_{i n}}\right]=x$, then subtracting equation (iii) from (ii) then,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left(x-0.1 \frac{R_F}{R_{\text {in }}}\right)-\left(x-1 \frac{R_F}{R_{\text {in }}}\right)=1-6 \\ & 0.9 \frac{R_F}{R_{\text {in }}}=-5 \\ & \quad \frac{R_F}{R_{\text {in }}}=-\frac{5}{0.9}=-5.55 \end{aligned} $$

content:
Consider the circuit with an ideal OPAMP shown in the figure.

Assuming $\left|V_{\mathrm{IN}}\right| \ll\left|V_{\mathrm{CC}}\right|$ and $\left|V_{\mathrm{REF}}\right| \ll\left|V_{\mathrm{CC}}\right|$. The condition at which $V_{\text {OUT }}$ equals to zero is
explanation:
For the ideal op-amp as shown in the given question, we have

On applying KCL at node $V^{-}$we get

$$ \frac{V_{\mathrm{IN}}-0}{R}+\frac{-V_{\mathrm{REF}}-0}{R}+\frac{V_{\mathrm{OUT}}-0}{R_{\mathrm{F}}}=0 . $$

or,  $$ \frac{V_{\mathrm{OUT}}}{R_{\mathrm{F}}}=\frac{1}{R}\left[V_{\mathrm{REF}}-V_{\mathrm{IN}}\right] $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{lrl} \text { As, } & V_{\text {OUT }}=0 \\ \therefore & V_{\text {REF }}-V_{\text {IN }}=0 \\ \text { And then we have } & V_{\text {IN }}=V_{\text {REF }} \end{array}\\ &\text { Hence, the correct option is (C). } \end{aligned} $$

content:
A circuit with an ideal OPAMP is shown in the figure. A plus $V_{\text {IN }}$ of 20 ms duration is applied to the input. The capacitors are initially uncharged.

The output voltage $V_{\text {OUT }}$ of this circuit at $\tau=0^{+}$(in integer) is $\_\_\_\_$ V.
explanation:
For the condition when we have $t=0^{+}$, the capacitor is replaced by a short circuit

We have,  $$ V^{-}=V_{\text {in }}=5 \mathrm{~V} $$

or,  $$ V^{+}=0 \mathrm{~V} $$

Now we also have, $\quad V^{-}>V^{+}$

So, $\quad V_{\text {out }}=-V_{\text {saf }}=-12 \mathrm{~V}$.

Hence, the correct answer is $(-12$ to -12$)$.

content: The components in the circuit shown below are ideal. If the op-amp is in positive feedback and the input voltage $V_i$ is a sine wave of amplitude 1 V , the output voltage $V_0$ is

options.content: a constant of either +5 V or -5 V .
a non-inverted sine wave of 2 V amplitude.
an inverted sine wave of 1 V amplitude.

explanation:
$$ \text { Given circuit is an non inverting Schmitt trigger, } $$

From given figure,

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_{N I}=\frac{V_i \times 1 \mathrm{k}}{1 \mathrm{k}+1 \mathrm{k}}+\frac{V_0 \times 1 \mathrm{k}}{1 \mathrm{k}+1 \mathrm{k}} \\ & V_{N I}=\frac{V_i+V_0}{2} \end{aligned} $$

and     $$ V_I=0 \mathrm{~V} $$

If $V_{N I}>0 \mathrm{~V}$ then $V_0=+V_{\text {sat }}$

If $V_{N I}<0 \mathrm{~V}$ then $V_0=-V_{\text {sat }}$

Assuming $V_0=+5 \mathrm{~V}=+V_{\text {sat }}$

For assumption to be true $V_{N I}$ must be greater than $V_I$,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{V_0+V_i}{2}>0 \mathrm{~V} \\ & V_0+V_i>0 \\ & V_i>-V_0 \\ & 1 \sin \omega t>-5 \mathrm{~V} \\ & V_0=+V_{\text {sat }}, \text { assumption is true } \end{aligned} $$

Now assuming $V_0=-V_{\text {sat }}=-5 \mathrm{~V}$

For the assumption to be true $V_{N I}$ must be less than $V_I$,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{V_0+V_i}{2}<0 \mathrm{~V} \\ & V_0+V_i<0 \mathrm{~V} \\ & V_i<-V_0 \\ & 1 \sin \omega t<+5 \quad\left[\because V_0=-5 \mathrm{~V}\right] \end{aligned} $$

The relation holds true in this case also. So the value of output may be possibly -5 V or +5 V as used. Hence the correct option is (A).

content: In the circuit shown below, all the components are ideal. If $V_i$ is +2 V , the current $I_0$ sourced by the OpAmp is $\_\_\_\_$ mA .
explanation:
$$ \text { Given Op-Amp circuit is shown in below figure, } $$

Since this is a-ve feedback Op-Amp circuit. So we can apply virtual grounded concept.

Here,  $$ V_x=V_i=2 \mathrm{~V} $$

Applying KCL at node $V_x$,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{V_x-0}{1 \mathrm{k}}+\frac{V_x-V_0}{1 \mathrm{k}}+0=0 \\ & \frac{2-0}{1 \mathrm{k}}+\frac{2-V_0}{1 \mathrm{k}}=0 \\ & V_0=4 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

Applying KCL at node $V_0$,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{V_0-V_x}{1 \mathrm{k}}+\frac{V_0-0}{1 \mathrm{k}}-I_0=0 \\ & \frac{4-2}{1 \mathrm{k}}+\frac{4}{1 \mathrm{k}}=I_0 \\ & I_0=6 \mathrm{~mA} \end{aligned} $$

content: The frequency compensation is used in OP-Amps to increase its___________

The opamps in the circuit shown are ideal, but have saturation voltages of ±10 V.

Assume that the initial inductor current is 0 A. The input voltage (Vi) is a triangular signal with peak voltages of ±2 V and time period of 8 μs. Which one of the following statements is true?

options.content: V01 is delayed by 2 μs relative to Vi, and V02 is a triangular waveform.
V01 is not delayed relative to Vi, and V02 is a trapezoidal waveform.
V01 is not delayed relative to Vi, and V02 is a triangular waveform.
V01 is delayed by 1 μs relative to Vi, and V02 is a trapezoidal waveform.

explanation:
$$ \text { For Op - Amp 1: } $$

The given Op-Amp circuit has a positive feedback. It is referred as Schmitt trigger circuit.

$V_{0_1}$ can be either $+V_{\text {Sat }}(+10 \mathrm{~V})$ or $-V_{\text {Sat }}(-10 \mathrm{~V})$

(1) Let us assume $V_{0_1}=+10$ Volt

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_{+}=\frac{V_{0_1}(10 K)+V_{\text {in }}(100 \mathrm{~K})}{110 \mathrm{~K}} \\ & V_{+}=\frac{V_{0_1}}{11}+\frac{10 V_{\text {in }}}{11} \\ & V_{+}=\frac{10}{11}+\frac{10 V_{\text {in }}}{11} \end{aligned} $$

First we have assumed the output to be +10 Volt. Now we have to write when it will go to $-V_{\text {sat }}(-10 \mathrm{~V})$.

When $V_{+} < V_{-} \Rightarrow V_{+}<0 \Rightarrow V_{0_1}=-10 \mathrm{~V}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{10}{11}+\frac{10 V_{i n}}{11}<0 \\ & V_{i n}<-1 \end{aligned} $$

When input voltage is less than -1 Volt at that time output will go to $-V_{s a t}$.

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { (2) Let us assume } V_{0_1}=-10 \text { Volt }\\ &\begin{aligned} & V_{+}=\frac{V_{0_1}}{11}+\frac{10 V_{i n}}{11} \\ & \text { When } V_{+}>V_{-} \Rightarrow V_{+}>0 \Rightarrow V_{0_1}=+10 \mathrm{~V} \\ & \frac{-10}{11}+\frac{10 V_{i n}}{11}>0 \Rightarrow V_{0_1}=+10 \mathrm{~V} \\ & V_{i n}>1 \mathrm{~V} \Rightarrow V_{0_1}=+10 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad V_{0_1}$ has $1 \mu \mathrm{sec}$ delay.

For Op - Amp 2 :

This is a integrator and we can write output $\left(V_{0_2}\right)$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_{\mathrm{O}_2}=\frac{-R}{L} \int_1^5 V_{\mathrm{O}_1} \mathrm{dt} \\ & V_{\mathrm{O}_2}=\frac{-10^3}{10^{-3}} \int_1^5 V_{\mathrm{O}_1} \mathrm{dt}=-10^6 \int_1^5 10 \mathrm{dt} \\ & V_{\mathrm{O}_2}=-10^6 \times 10 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \end{aligned} $$

Change in $V_{0_2}=+40$ Volt

We get change of 40 volt but practically not possible because highest voltage is 20 volt. That is why it will saturate so we are not going to get the perfect triangular waveform. We will get trapezoidal waveform.

$$ \text { Hence the correct option is (D). } $$

content:
In the circuit below, the opamp is ideal.

If the circuit is to show sustained oscillations, the respective values of $R_1$ and the corresponding frequency of oscillation are ____.
explanation:
$$ \text { Given, circuit is shown below, } $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Applying Nodal Analysis. }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \frac{V_1-V_0}{\left(\frac{1}{s C}\right)}+\frac{V_1}{R}+\frac{V_1-V_{+}}{R}=0 \\ & s C\left(V_1-V_0\right)+\frac{\left(2 V_1-V_{+}\right)}{R}=0 \\ & s C R\left(V_1-V_0\right)+2 V_1-V_{+}=0 \\ & V_1(2+s C R)-V_{+}-s C R V_0=0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { At Nodal }\left(V_{+}\right) \text {. }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \frac{V_{+}}{\left(\frac{1}{s C}\right)}+\frac{V_{+}-V_1}{R}=0 \\ & s C\left(V_{+}\right)+\frac{\left(V_{+}-V_1\right)}{R}=0 \\ & s C R\left(V_{+}\right)+V_{+}-V_1=0 \\ & V_1=V_{+}(1+S C R) \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { The Value of } V_1 \text { put in equation (i) and we get. }\\ &\begin{aligned} & V_{+}(1+s C R)(2+s C R)-V_{+}=s C R V_0 \\ & V_{+}((1+s C R)(2+s C R)-1)=V_0 s C R \\ & V_{+}=\frac{V_0(s C R)}{[(1+s C R)(2+s C R)]-1} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { From given circuit, voltage gain is given by. }\\ &\begin{aligned} \frac{V_0}{V_{+}} & =\left(1+\frac{R_1}{R}\right) \\ \frac{V_0}{V_{+}} & =\frac{[(1+s C R)(2+s C R)]-1}{s C R} \\ \frac{V_0}{V_{+}} & =\frac{(1+s C R)(2+s C R)-1}{s C R} \\ \frac{V_0}{V_{+}} & =\frac{2+s C R+2 s C R+s^2 C^2 R^2-1}{s C R} \\ \frac{V_0}{V_{+}} & =\frac{2+3 s C R+s^2 C^2 R^2-1}{s C R} \\ \frac{V_0}{V_{+}} & =\frac{2}{j \omega C R}+3+j \omega C R-\frac{1}{j \omega C R} \\ \frac{V_0}{V_{+}} & =\frac{1}{j \omega C R}+3+j \omega C R \\ \frac{V_0}{V_{+}} & =\frac{1+3 j \omega C R+j^2 \omega^2 C^2 R^2}{j \omega C R} \\ \frac{V_0}{V_{+}} & =\frac{1+3 j \omega C R-\omega^2 C^2 R^2}{j \omega C R} \\ \frac{V_0}{V_{+}} & =\left[\frac{1-\omega^2 C^2 R^2}{j \omega C R}+3\right] \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \frac{V_0}{V_{+}}=\left(\frac{1-\omega^2 C^2 R^2}{j \omega C R}+3\right)=\left(1+\frac{R_1}{R}\right) \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots(iii) $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & 1-\omega^2 C^2 R^2=0 \\ & \omega=\frac{1}{R C} \\ & f=\frac{1}{2 \pi R C} \end{aligned} $$

From equation (iii). For sustained oscillation.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left(1+\frac{R_1}{R}\right)=3 \\ & \frac{R_1}{R}=2 \\ & R_1=2 R \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content: The $$\mathrm{{{{V_{OUT}}} \over {{V_{IN}}}}}$$ of the circuit shown below is
explanation:
Given circuit can be drawn as shown below

From the above figure, $V_{01}=-\frac{R_2}{R_1} \times V_{\text {in }}$ ........(i)

$$ V_{02}=\left(1+\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) V_{\text {in }} $$ ........(ii)

$\begin{aligned} & \therefore V_{\text {out }}=-\frac{R_4}{R_3} V_{01}-\frac{R_4}{R_3} V_{02} \\\\ & V_{\text {out }}=\frac{-R_4}{R_3}\left[V_{01}+V_{02}\right] ........(ii)\end{aligned}$

Substituting equations (i) and (ii) in equation (iii), we get

$$ V_{\text {out }}=\frac{-R_4}{R_3}\left[\frac{-R_2}{R_1} V_{\text {in }}+V_{\text {in }}+\frac{R_2}{R_1} V_{\text {in }}\right]=\frac{-R_4}{R_3} V_{\text {in }} \Rightarrow \frac{V_{\text {out }}}{V_{\text {in }}}=\frac{-R_4}{R_3} $$

Hence, the correct option is (A).

content: A sample and hold circuit is implemented using a resistive switch and a capacitor with a time constant of 1 $$\mu$$s. The time for the sampling switch to stay closed to charge a capacitor adequately to a full scale voltage of 1 V with 12-bit accuracy is ___________ $$\mu$$s (rounded off to two decimal places).
explanation:
To determine the time required for the sampling switch to stay closed and charge the capacitor to a full-scale voltage of 1 V with 12-bit accuracy, we need to consider the time constant and the accuracy level.

The given time constant $$\tau$$ of the circuit is 1 $$\mu$$s. The voltage across a charging capacitor in an RC circuit as a function of time can be described by the exponential function:

$$ V(t) = V_{max} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right) $$

Here, $$V(t)$$ is the voltage across the capacitor at time $$t$$, $$V_{max}$$ is the maximum voltage (1 V in this case), and $$\tau$$ is the time constant (1 $$\mu$$s in this case).

We need the capacitor to charge to a voltage within 12-bit accuracy of the full-scale voltage. The number of bits (12) determines the resolution. The accuracy needed can be given by:

$$ Accuracy = V_{max} \times \left( \frac{1}{2^{12}} \right) $$

$$ Accuracy = 1V \times \left( \frac{1}{4096} \right) $$

$$ Accuracy = \frac{1}{4096}V $$

The voltage deviation allowed due to 12-bit accuracy is:

$$ 1 V - \frac{1}{4096} V $$

To find the time required, we can set up the equation by incorporating the allowed voltage deviation:

$$ 1 - e^{-t/\tau} = 1 - \frac{1}{4096} $$

This simplifies to:

$$ e^{-t/\tau} = \frac{1}{4096} $$

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides to solve for $$t$$:

$$ -\frac{t}{\tau} = \ln\left( \frac{1}{4096} \right) $$

$$ t = -\tau \cdot \ln\left( \frac{1}{4096} \right) $$

Substituting the values of $$\tau = 1 \mu s$$:

$$ t = -1 \mu s \cdot \ln\left( \frac{1}{4096} \right) $$

Using properties of logarithms:

$$ \ln\left( \frac{1}{4096} \right) = \ln(1) - \ln(4096) = 0 - \ln(4096) $$

Since $$\ln(4096) = \ln(2^{12}) = 12 \cdot \ln(2) \approx 12 \cdot 0.693 = 8.316$$:

Thus:

$$ t = 1 \mu s \cdot 8.316 $$

$$ t \approx 8.32 \mu s $$

Therefore, the time for the sampling switch to stay closed to charge the capacitor adequately to a full-scale voltage of 1 V with 12-bit accuracy is approximately 8.32 $$\mu$$s (rounded off to two decimal places).

content: A circuit with an ideal OPAMP is shown. The Bode plot for the magnitude (in dB) of the gain transfer function (Av(j$$\omega$$) = Vout(j$$\omega$$)/Vin(j$$\omega$$)) of the circuit is also provided (here, $$\omega$$ is the angular frequency in rad/s). The values of R and C are __________.
explanation:
Maximum gain = 12 dB

20 $$\times$$ log Amax = 12

Amax = 4

$$1 + {{{R_2}} \over {{R_1}}} = 4 \Rightarrow {R_2} = 3{R_1}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ R = 3 $$\times$$ 1 = 3 k$$\Omega$$

log10 $$\omega$$c = 3 $$\Rightarrow$$ $$\omega$$c = 1000 rad/sec

$${\omega _c} = {1 \over {{R_3}C}} \Rightarrow C = {1 \over {{R_3} \times {\omega _c}}}$$

$$C = {1 \over {1000 \times 1000}} = 1$$ $$\mu$$F
